extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A thermal incubator system comprising an insulated styrofoam container (0.03 m³ volume), a heatbed (214×214×3 mm) for uniform heating, a fan (0.02 m³/s airflow) for air circulation, two internal temperature sensors (DS18S20, -55°C to +125°C accuracy 0.5°C), and one external temperature sensor. The system regulates temperature within the insulated container regardless of internal content, with a heatbed capable of delivering 100W power (200J in 2s) to raise air temperature from 293K (20°C) to 300K (26.85°C). The physical twin (PT) operates as a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) with cyber components (controller, sensors) and physical components (heatbed, fan).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Monitoring (real-time temperature data collection via sensors), optimization (calibration algorithms for model parameter estimation), prediction (simulation models with 2/4 free parameters for thermal inertia analysis), control (Raspberry Pi-based controller adjusting heatbed/fan via RabbitMQ), and visualization (software system for data delivery and simulation). The DT enables predictive behavior analysis of the PT under operational conditions and supports fault detection through algorithmic data manipulation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (GPIO for sensor/actuator control, Wi-Fi/Bluetooth), RabbitMQ (message broker for controller-software communication), DS18S20 1-Wire temperature sensors (64-bit serial codes for multi-sensor bus), MOSFETs with optocouplers (isolation for 4A heatbed current), PCB schematic (component connections), simulation models (2-parameter and 4-parameter thermal inertia prediction), and open-source repository (GitHub: https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) for hardware/software specifications.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization between physical and digital components via RabbitMQ message passing (commands and control states shared in real-time). Calibration experiments use data trajectories with temporal granularity for model parameter estimation. Kalman filter operates in real-time for anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation. Self-adaptation loop includes dynamic recalibration of Model B with latency tied to experiment scheduling (e.g., cooling periods and heating ramps).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Two distinct simulation models (Model A: 2 parameters, Model B: 4 parameters) with hierarchical calibration. Centralized control architecture via Raspberry Pi controller, with decentralized sensor nodes (3 temperature sensors, fan, heatbed). Coordination mechanisms include RabbitMQ message brokers for distributed component communication. Model B instance handles heatbed inertia prediction with higher fidelity.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated system integrating: 1) RabbitMQ as central message bus for controller-low level driver communication, 2) Kalman filter for state estimation, 3) Calibration algorithms updating model parameters, 4) Time series database for dashboard visualization. Self-adaptation loop closes the DT loop by reconfiguring controllers based on new experiment data. Architecture patterns include event-driven communication and model-based simulation.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration via RabbitMQ AMQP protocol for cross-component communication. Open-source repository (GitHub) enables interoperability with external tools. DT connects to physical twin through sensor networks and uses standard data exchange protocols. Potential integration with enterprise systems via API interfaces for anomaly detection and predictive maintenance workflows.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include behavioral and analytical models (e.g., Model A with 2 parameters: C_air=616.56, G_box=0.65; Model B with 4 parameters: C_air=486.12, G_box=0.856, C_heater=33.65, G_heater=0.87). Data sources are sensors on the Physical Twin (PT) and historical data. Data management uses time series databases for real-time visualization. Models are calibrated via nonlinear least squares optimization (Scipy package) to minimize residual error. Model B provides higher fidelity for thermal inertia prediction. Algorithms include Kalman filters for state estimation and what-if simulations using co-simulations with PT data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators include a heatbed (controlled via MOSFETs for 4A current) and a fan. Control mechanisms use Raspberry Pi GPIO with optocouplers to isolate control signals. Actuation ranges: heatbed power regulation via voltage (18V tested), fan speed control. Safety constraints include overcurrent protection for MOSFETs. Remote control interfaces: GPIO pins on Raspberry Pi. Addressing mechanisms: unique 64-bit serial codes for sensors, direct hardware connections for actuators.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors: DS18S20 1-Wire Digital Thermometers (3 units) with ±0.5°C accuracy (-10°C to +85°C). Measurement capabilities: temperature (-55°C to +125°C). Spatial distribution: three sensors placed within the incubator chamber. Sampling frequency: not explicitly stated. Data transmission: 1-Wire bus with serial communication. Data collection mechanisms: low-level drivers interfacing with RabbitMQ for real-time data streaming.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity requires calibration using experimental data trajectories and parameter optimization (Scipy's nonlinear least squares). Validation methods: compare model outputs (e.g., heatbed temperature) with sensor data from experiments. Uncertainty sources: parameter estimation errors, sensor noise. Error detection: Kalman filters for anomaly detection in heatbed state estimation. Quality assurance: iterative calibration experiments, model validation against real-world data, and residual error minimization (targeting <1% error margins).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (DS18S20 temperature sensors) to digital twin via 1-Wire protocol. Data types include temperature readings (-55°C to +125°C with 0.5°C accuracy). Communication protocol: 1-Wire for sensors, RabbitMQ for system-wide data transmission. Transmission frequency: Continuous monitoring with periodic updates (exact interval unspecified). Event triggers: Temperature thresholds or manual calibration events. Data quality assurance: Calibration experiments (Model A/B parameters) and sensor validation. Preprocessing: Data normalization and filtering for noise reduction. Data integration: Aggregation via RabbitMQ server and time-series database for visualization.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (fan speed, heatbed power) sent from digital twin to physical components via RabbitMQ. Command types: Setpoint adjustments, calibration triggers. Control message format: JSON-based commands through RabbitMQ exchange. Validation: Command authenticity checks via RabbitMQ message acknowledgment. Feedback systems: Real-time temperature sensor data feeding back to controller. Conflict resolution: Prioritization of emergency shutdown commands over regular adjustments. Command execution confirmation: Status updates from hardware (e.g., fan operational feedback).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication protocols: 1-Wire (sensors), RabbitMQ (system-wide messaging). Network topology: Edge (Raspberry Pi) + cloud (RabbitMQ server). Bandwidth requirements: Low (RabbitMQ is lightweight). Latency: Minimal due to direct GPIO control and local processing. Reliability: Message acknowledgments in RabbitMQ, redundant sensor data collection. Security: Basic authentication for RabbitMQ, no explicit encryption mentioned. Network architecture: Client-server model with Raspberry Pi as central controller.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Hosting platforms: Edge (Raspberry Pi 4 Model B) and cloud (RabbitMQ server). Computational requirements: Raspberry Pi 4 (4-core Cortex-A53, 1.5GHz). Storage: Local storage on Raspberry Pi for time-series data. Scalability: Modular design allows adding sensors/actuators. Deployment models: Standalone edge deployment with optional cloud integration. Availability: High via Raspberry Pi's reliability and RabbitMQ uptime. Redundancy: Dual temperature sensors for fault tolerance.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle includes development stages such as simulation model calibration (Model A with 2 parameters, Model B with 4 parameters), real-time visualization deployment via time series databases, and self-adaptation loops triggered by environmental changes (e.g., adding cold water). Lifecycle governance involves continuous calibration, model reconfiguration (e.g., recalibrating Model B parameters after experiments), and version control through open-source repositories.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering processes include model calibration experiments (e.g., optimizing sensor setups, generating data trajectories), RabbitMQ-based communication for real-time data synchronization, and Kalman filter integration for state estimation. Evolution strategies involve dynamic recalibration of Model B parameters, what-if simulations for controller optimization, and iterative testing via co-simulations with historical/real-time data. Version control is achieved through open-source hardware/software availability.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support includes anomaly detection via Kalman filters correlating sensor data to heatbed temperatures, heatbed state estimation, and what-if simulations for controller parameter optimization. Analytics capabilities involve differential equation-based dynamic models and real-time data visualization dashboards. User interaction includes alerts for system violations and manual/controller reconfiguration steps in self-adaptation loops.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with open-source standards (RabbitMQ, 1-Wire protocols) and interoperability frameworks for sensor networks (DS18S20 temperature sensors). Certification requirements include open-source hardware/software availability and adherence to CPS modeling standards for thermal inertia prediction. Industry specifications follow CPS frameworks for cyber-physical system integration.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

Although the dynamics models have been built, they contain free parameters. In order

derived above. Then the models are evaluated using the estimated parameters. Afterwards the resulting behaviour is compared with a data trajectory that is obtained by
running an experiment of the incubator. Finally using an optimisation function inside
the python Scipy package [14] to obtain the values of the parameters. Such a function
finds the parameters that minimizes the residual error. When the residual error is small
enough, the optimised parameters can be obtained.
The principle of the package is based on least-square, it assumes that:


_y_ = _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) + _ϵ_ (10)


where _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) is the model needed to be calibrated, _θ_ are the parameters represented in
a vector in the model, and _y_ is the label or the measured data.
A cost function or an objective function can be provided as:


_J_ = �( _y_ _i_ _−_ _f_ ( _x_ _i_ _, θ_ )) [2] (11)


The objective is to acquire a minimal value of _J_, and at the same time, the corresponding _θ_ are the value needed. In order to minimise the cost function, the most commonly
used one is gradient descent. After multiple iterations, the value of the cost function
goes to a sufficient small value and the _θ_ are obtained simultaneously.

makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

processes [1].

directly be controlled.

wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

**3.3** **Calibration**


Although the dynamics models have been built, they contain free parameters. In order
to utilise the models, the models need to be calibrated for the incubator. The results of
the calibration are detailed in section 4.2, and here we detail the procedure.
A non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate the parameters of the models
derived above. Then the models are evaluated using the estimated parameters. Afterwards the resulting behaviour is compared with a data trajectory that is obtained by
running an experiment of the incubator. Finally using an optimisation function inside
the python Scipy package [14] to obtain the values of the parameters. Such a function
finds the parameters that minimizes the residual error. When the residual error is small
enough, the optimised parameters can be obtained.
The principle of the package is based on least-square, it assumes that:


_y_ = _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) + _ϵ_ (10)


where _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) is the model needed to be calibrated, _θ_ are the parameters represented in
a vector in the model, and _y_ is the label or the measured data.
A cost function or an objective function can be provided as:


_J_ = �( _y_ _i_ _−_ _f_ ( _x_ _i_ _, θ_ )) [2] (11)


The objective is to acquire a minimal value of _J_, and at the same time, the corresponding _θ_ are the value needed. In order to minimise the cost function, the most commonly
used one is gradient descent. After multiple iterations, the value of the cost function
goes to a sufficient small value and the _θ_ are obtained simultaneously.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C from -10 to +85 °C, which is suitable for the physical incubator. In order to decouple the control signals from the Raspberry Pi to the MOSFETs, we use optocouplers. The PCB schematic is shown in fig. 4.
